output_summary: aligned schemas for datasets: dbpedia, metacritic, sales; generated 2 schema correspondence groups.
profiled datasets: dbpedia, metacritic, sales.
Reused the blocking configuration from state.
Blocking uses `semantic_similarity` for dbpedia_sales, metacritic_dbpedia, metacritic_sales, blocking on name, platform, releaseYear.
Performance-wise, pair completeness ranges from 0.91 to 1.00 and candidate counts range from 409504 to 929573.
The config also records dataset id columns.

matcher mode `rule_based` with config source reused from state; evaluated pairs: id_columns, matching_strategies; selected scores: matching_strategies (threshold=0.75, f1=0.864367816091954, threshold=0.8, f1=0.8866995073891625, threshold=0.78); produced 2 pair strategy entries.
This run created an initial draft of the pipeline.
The pipeline was built to load the schema-matched dbpedia, metacritic, and sales CSV files from output/schema-matching/.

It added three pairwise EmbeddingBlocker configurations for dbpedia-sales, metacritic-dbpedia, and metacritic-sales using the sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 model with sklearn indexing and top_k=20.
It added three rule-based matching configurations with pair-specific comparator sets, weights, and thresholds, and wrote the resulting correspondences to output/correspondences/.
It added a data fusion stage that concatenates all pairwise correspondences and fuses 10 attributes, then writes fused output to output/data_fusion/fusion_data.csv.
Fusion behavior is centered on longest_string, which is used for 7 of the 10 fused attributes including name, releaseYear, developer, platform, series, publisher, and ESRB.
Numeric-style fusion is limited to maximum for criticScore, userScore, and globalSales.
Matching behavior is pair-specific rather than uniform, with different comparator mixes, weights, and thresholds for each dataset pair.
The pipeline uses a lowercasing and trimming preprocess helper for string comparators when values are not null.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 1; fusion_data.csv was produced.
This run created an initial draft of the evaluation script for the fused output.

The run evaluated fused records by first building an eval_id-aligned view from fusion sources and then applying column-specific match functions through a rule-based DataFusionStrategy and DataFusionEvaluator.
Name, developer, publisher, platform, and ESRB were evaluated with tokenized_match.
releaseYear was evaluated with year_only_match.
criticScore and userScore were evaluated with numeric_tolerance_match.
Detected list-like non-metadata columns were normalized across fused and gold data and then evaluated with set_equality_match.
The run added _parse_source_ids to parse _fusion_sources values from native collections or serialized JSON and Python literals.
The run added _detect_gold_prefix to infer the dominant gold ID prefix and use it when mapping fused source IDs to gold evaluation IDs.
The run added build_eval_view to expand fused rows into eval_id candidates, preserve cluster IDs, sort by _fusion_confidence when present, and deduplicate by eval_id.
The run added a fallback that uses fused _id as eval_id and _eval_cluster_id when no expanded evaluation rows can be built.
evaluation execution succeeded on attempt 1; pipeline_evaluation.json is available.
overall accuracy is 29.41%; evaluation attempt counter is 1.
This run was a refinement of the existing evaluation pipeline.
The pipeline added dataset-wide normalization before matching, including generic text cleanup, releaseYear date normalization, and platform alias standardization.
The matching thresholds were raised for all three pairwise matchers from 0.75 to 0.78 for dbpedia-sales, from 0.8 to 0.82 for metacritic-dbpedia, and from 0.78 to 0.82 for metacritic-sales.
Fusion behavior was changed to include singleton records by setting include_singletons to True.
The pipeline added post-fusion cleanup for releaseYear, platform, name, developer, publisher, and ESRB before writing the fused output.
The run shifted toward a more conservative matching strategy by tightening all rule-based match thresholds.
The run also shifted fusion coverage toward retaining unmatched records through singleton inclusion instead of emitting only matched clusters.
The attribute fusion strategy itself did not change, and it still relies primarily on longest_string with maximum for numeric score and sales fields.
The run introduced helper functions lower_strip, normalize_text, normalize_year_date, and normalize_platform to standardize values across all three source datasets.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 1; fusion_data.csv was produced.
This run was an evaluation refinement rather than an initial draft or repair.
The dominant strategy was to keep the same field-specific evaluation functions while refining evaluation execution and aligning the evaluator to the correct fusion strategy name.

The evaluation still used tokenized matching for name, developer, publisher, platform, and ESRB, year-only matching for releaseYear, numeric tolerance matching for criticScore and userScore, and set equality for detected list-like fields.
Textual identity and entity fields were grouped under tokenized_match, including name, developer, publisher, platform, and ESRB.
Temporal fields were handled separately with year_only_match for releaseYear.
Numeric score fields were grouped under numeric_tolerance_match for criticScore and userScore.
Any detected list-like fields not already assigned were grouped under set_equality_match.
The run added a print_structured_metrics helper to print nested evaluation results in a structured way instead of dumping formatted JSON to stdout.
The evaluator was switched from DataFusionStrategy("rule_based_fusion_strategy_eval") to DataFusionStrategy("rule_based_fusion_strategy").
evaluation execution succeeded on attempt 1; pipeline_evaluation.json is available.
overall accuracy is 35.29%; previous logged accuracy was 29.41%; evaluation attempt counter is 2.
This run was an evaluation-driven refinement of the existing pipeline.
The matching thresholds were relaxed from 0.78 to 0.75 for dbpedia-sales, from 0.82 to 0.80 for metacritic-dbpedia, and from 0.82 to 0.78 for metacritic-sales.
The fusion strategy changed developer and series from longest_string to union.
The fusion strategy changed criticScore, userScore, and globalSales from maximum to longest_string.
The fusion engine was changed to run with include_singletons set to false instead of true.
The pipeline shifted toward looser entity matching by lowering all three rule-based match thresholds.
The fusion behavior shifted away from numeric maximum selection for score and sales fields and toward longest_string selection.
The fusion behavior shifted developer and series aggregation toward multi-value preservation through union rather than picking a single longest value.
Normalization was tightened so invalid text and unparsable dates now become null values instead of being retained.
Platform normalization was expanded with additional aliases such as x-box 360, x-box one, gba, gbc, and gc.
ESRB normalization was added to standardize labels such as Everyone, Teen, Mature, and related variants.

rb_fused_standard_blocker = engine.run(; fusion_data.csv was produced.
This run was an execution repair that reworked the pipeline after the previous engine.run failure.
The matching thresholds were raised for all three pairwise matches, from 0.75 to 0.82 for dbpedia-sales, from 0.80 to 0.85 for metacritic-dbpedia, and from 0.78 to 0.83 for metacritic-sales.
A new deduplicate_pairwise_correspondences helper was added to prune many-to-many links by keeping the highest-scoring match per left and right id before fusion.

The fusion strategy was substantially revised so that name now uses best_name, releaseYear, developer, platform, and ESRB use most_frequent_non_null, publisher uses first_non_null, and criticScore, userScore, and globalSales use maximum instead of longest_string or union.
The pipeline shifted from a more permissive correspondence and string-length-driven fusion approach to a more conservative one-to-one matching and value-selection approach.

Fusion behavior moved away from longest_string and union toward consensus-based categorical selection and numeric maxima, which favors stable canonical values over concatenated or arbitrarily long text.
The code now imports maximum from PyDI.fusion and defines new helper fusers first_non_null, most_frequent_non_null, and best_name.
pipeline execution succeeded on attempt 2; fusion_data.csv was produced.
This run was a refinement of the evaluation logic in evaluation_refinement mode rather than an initial draft or a repair.

The evaluation kept a field-specific strategy that used tokenized matching for textual game attributes, year-only matching for releaseYear, numeric-tolerance matching for score and sales fields, and set-equality matching for detected list-like columns.
Name, developer, publisher, platform, and ESRB were grouped under tokenized_match as text-oriented fields.
ReleaseYear was evaluated with year_only_match as a date-specific field.
CriticScore, userScore, and the newly added globalSales were grouped under numeric_tolerance_match as numeric fields.
Any detected list-like non-system columns not already assigned were evaluated with set_equality_match.
The import fallback for list_normalization was hardened by guarding __file__ access with globals checks and falling back to Path.cwd() when needed.
List-normalization execution now prints an explicit 'none' message when no list-like columns are detected.
The strategy name was changed from rule_based_fusion_strategy to games_fusion_evaluation_strategy.
Structured result printing was simplified to direct JSON dumping instead of a custom print_structured_metrics helper.
evaluation execution succeeded on attempt 1; pipeline_evaluation.json is available.
